q: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> is connected to a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery through a resistor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and a switch. After a long time, when maximum current is set up in the circuit, the current is switched off. The current in the circuit after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μs</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to ______ . (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>37</mn></math>)
question: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> is connected to a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery through a resistor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and a switch. After a long time, when maximum current is set up in the circuit, the current is switched off. The current in the circuit after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μs</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to ______ . (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>37</mn></math>)
solution:
Imax=VR=20 V10 KΩ=2 mA

For LR-decay circuit, 

I=Imaxe-Rt/L

I=2 mA e-10×103×1×10-610×10-3

I=2 mA e-1

I=2×0.37 mA

I=74100 mA

x=74

subject: Physics
q: An alternating current is given by $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}} \cos \omega \mathrm{t}$. The r.m.s current will be
question: An alternating current is given by $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}} \cos \omega \mathrm{t}$. The r.m.s current will be
solution: $\begin{aligned} & I=I_{\mathrm{A}} \sin \omega t+I_B \cos \omega t \\ & I_{\mathrm{ma}}=\sqrt{I_A^2+I_B^2} \\ & \text { So, } I_{\mathrm{RMS}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_A^2+I_B^2}{2}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An inductor stores 16 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates 32 W of thermal energy due to its resistance when an a.c. current of 2 A (rms) and frequency 50 Hz flows through it. The ratio of inductive reactance to its resistance is $\_\_\_\_$. $(\pi=3.14)$
question: An inductor stores 16 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates 32 W of thermal energy due to its resistance when an a.c. current of 2 A (rms) and frequency 50 Hz flows through it. The ratio of inductive reactance to its resistance is $\_\_\_\_$. $(\pi=3.14)$
solution:
Given: Magnetic field energy $= 16$ J, Thermal power dissipated $= 32$ W, $I_{rms} = 2$ A, $f = 50$ Hz.
From energy stored in inductor:
$\frac{1}{2}L I_{rms}^2 = 16$
$\frac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot 4 = 16$
$L = 8 \text{ H}$
From thermal power dissipated:
$I_{rms}^2 R = 32$
$4R = 32$
$R = 8 \; \Omega$
Inductive reactance:
$X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 8 = 2512 \; \Omega$
$\frac{X_L}{R} = \frac{2512}{8} = 314$

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of $10$ mH, capacitor of $0.1\ \mu$F and a resistor of $100\ \Omega$ are connected in series across an $a.c$ power supply $220$ V, $70$ Hz. The power factor of the given circuit is $0.5$. The difference in the inductive reactance and capacitance reactance is $\sqrt{3}\alpha\ \Omega$. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
question: An inductor of $10$ mH, capacitor of $0.1\ \mu$F and a resistor of $100\ \Omega$ are connected in series across an $a.c$ power supply $220$ V, $70$ Hz. The power factor of the given circuit is $0.5$. The difference in the inductive reactance and capacitance reactance is $\sqrt{3}\alpha\ \Omega$. The value of $\alpha$ is _____.
solution:
The power factor of an LCR series circuit is given by $\cos \phi = \dfrac{R}{Z}$.

Given $\cos \phi = 0.5$ and $R = 100 \ \Omega$.

Substituting the values, we get:

$0.5 = \dfrac{100}{Z} \Rightarrow Z = 200 \ \Omega$

The impedance $Z$ of the circuit is also given by:

$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$

Squaring both sides and substituting the values of $Z$ and $R$:

$(200)^2 = (100)^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2$

$40000 = 10000 + (X_L - X_C)^2$

$(X_L - X_C)^2 = 30000$

$|X_L - X_C| = 100\sqrt{3} \ \Omega$

The difference in inductive and capacitive reactance is given as $\sqrt{3}\alpha \ \Omega$.

Comparing the two expressions:

$\sqrt{3}\alpha = 100\sqrt{3}$

$\alpha = 100$

Answer: $100$

subject: Physics
q: A LCR circuit is at resonance for a capacitor $C$, inductance $L$ and resistance $R$. Now the value of resistance is halved keeping all other parameters same. The current amplitude at resonance will be now:
question: A LCR circuit is at resonance for a capacitor $C$, inductance $L$ and resistance $R$. Now the value of resistance is halved keeping all other parameters same. The current amplitude at resonance will be now:
solution:
In resonance $Z=R$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \text { halved } \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}\end{aligned}$
I becomes doubled.

subject: Physics
q: In series LCR circuit, the capacitance is changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>C</mi></math>. To keep the resonance frequency unchanged, the new inductance should be :
question: In series LCR circuit, the capacitance is changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>C</mi></math>. To keep the resonance frequency unchanged, the new inductance should be :
solution:
As resonance frequency remains unchanged, therefore ω'=ω

⇒1L'C'=1LC

⇒ L'C'=LC

⇒L'4C=LC

⇒L'=L4

∵ Inductance must be decreased by 3L4.

If the current in the primary coil of the transformer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and its efficiency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>%</mi></math>, the output current would be:
question: A power transmission line feeds input power at<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>2300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> to a step-down transformer with its primary windings having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4000</mn><mo> </mo></math>turns giving the output power at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. If the current in the primary coil of the transformer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and its efficiency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>%</mi></math>, the output current would be:
solution:
Efficiency, η=0.9=PsPp

VsIs=0.9 × VpIp

Is=0.9 ×2300 ×5230=45 A

question: The time taken for the magnetic energy to reach <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of its maximum value, when a solenoid of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is connected to a battery, is :

subject: Physics
q: Two identical capacitors A and B, charged to the same potential 5V are connected in two different circuits as shown below at time t=0. If the charge on capacitors A and B at time t=CR is QA and QB respectively, then (Here e is the base of natural logarithm)
question: Two identical capacitors A and B, charged to the same potential 5V are connected in two different circuits as shown below at time t=0. If the charge on capacitors A and B at time t=CR is QA and QB respectively, then (Here e is the base of natural logarithm)
solution:
Charge on capacitor after charging with a battery of potential V, Q=CV

Now, capacitor A is connected as shown in the figure.

As diode is in reverse bias, so capacitor charge remains same after time t=CR.

∴ QA=CV

In case B, capacitor is connected as shown in the figure.

Here, diode is in forward bias, so capacitor discharges and its charge decays as 

QB=Q·e-tRC

where, t=RC

subject: Physics
q: The current flowing through an ac circuit is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>120</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. How long will the current take to reach the peak value starting from zero?
question: The current flowing through an ac circuit is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>120</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. How long will the current take to reach the peak value starting from zero?
solution:
If initial value of current is zero, then it will take T4 time to reach the peak value, where T= time period.

As we know, ω=120π

Therefore,

t=T4=2πω4  =π2ω=π2×120π=1240 s

question: As shown in the figure, a battery of emf ∈ is connected to an inductor L and resistance R in series. The switch is closed at t=0. The total charge that flows from the battery, between t=0 and t=tc ( tc is the time constant of the circuit) is:
solution:
q=∫0Tcidt
=∈Rt-e-tTc-1Tc0Tc=∈RTc+Tce-1-Tc
=∈R×1e×LR

 q=∈LR2e

subject: Physics
q: In an LC oscillator, if values of inductance and capacitance become twice and eight times, respectively, then the resonant frequency of oscillator becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> times its initial resonant frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
question: In an LC oscillator, if values of inductance and capacitance become twice and eight times, respectively, then the resonant frequency of oscillator becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> times its initial resonant frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
solution:
Resonance frequency of an LC oscillations circuit is stated as 

ω0=1LC, here, L and C represents inductance and capacitance of circuit.

Now, when inductance and capacitance changes, i.e., L'=2L and C'=8C

Then, resonant frequency is ω=12L×8C=14LC

⇒ω=ω04

Thus, the value of x=14.

q: In <math><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></math> circuit the inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> and capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If a voltage <math><mi>V</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>314</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is applied to the circuit, the current in the circuit is given as:
question: In <math><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></math> circuit the inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> and capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If a voltage <math><mi>V</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>314</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is applied to the circuit, the current in the circuit is given as:
solution:
z=R2+XC-XL2
R=0
Z=XC-XL
=1ωC-ωL
=1314×100×10-6-314×40×10-3
=31.84-12.56
=19.28 Ω

i=V0Zsin⁡314t+π2
∴i=V0Zcos⁡314t⇒i=1019.28cos⁡314t
⇒i=0.52cos⁡314t

subject: Physics
q: For ac circuit shown in figure, $\mathrm{R}=100 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ and $\mathrm{C}=100 \mathrm{pF}$ and the phase difference between $\mathrm{V}_{\text {in }}$ and $\left(V_B-V_A\right)$ is $90^{\circ}$. The input signal frequency is $10^x \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$, where ' x ' is ______
question: For ac circuit shown in figure, $\mathrm{R}=100 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ and $\mathrm{C}=100 \mathrm{pF}$ and the phase difference between $\mathrm{V}_{\text {in }}$ and $\left(V_B-V_A\right)$ is $90^{\circ}$. The input signal frequency is $10^x \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$, where ' x ' is ______
solution:
$\theta+\theta=90^{\circ} ; \theta=45^{\circ}$
$\tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\mathrm{R}}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{R} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\mathrm{R}$
$\mathrm{W}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{1}{100 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}$
$=\frac{10^{12}}{10^7}=10^5$

subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.

  List-I   List-II
(A) AC generator (I) Detects the presence of current in the circuit
(B) Galvanometer (II) Converts mechanical energy into electrical energy
(C) Transformer (III) Works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit
(D) Metal detector (IV) Changes an alternating voltage for smaller or greater value
question:
Match List - I with List - II.

  List-I   List-II
(A) AC generator (I) Detects the presence of current in the circuit
(B) Galvanometer (II) Converts mechanical energy into electrical energy
(C) Transformer (III) Works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit
(D) Metal detector (IV) Changes an alternating voltage for smaller or greater value
solution:
AC generator-- Converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. A→II.

Galvanometer-- Detects the presence of current in the circuit B-I.

Transformer-- Changes an alternating voltage for smaller or greater value C-IV.

Metal detector-- Works on the principle of resonance in AC circuit D-III.

subject: Physics
q: In the circuit shown here, the point 'C' is kept connected to point A '' till the current flowing through the circuit becomes constant. Afterward, suddenly, point 'C' is disconnected from point A '' and connected to point 'B' at time t=0. Ratio of the voltage across resistance and the inductor at t=LR will be equal to :
question: In the circuit shown here, the point 'C' is kept connected to point A '' till the current flowing through the circuit becomes constant. Afterward, suddenly, point 'C' is disconnected from point A '' and connected to point 'B' at time t=0. Ratio of the voltage across resistance and the inductor at t=LR will be equal to :
solution:
Applying Kirchhoff's law in closed loop,

-VR-VL=0

⇒ VR  VL =- 1

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance is connected across <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> AC supply. The time taken by the current to change from its maximum value to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rms</mi></math> value is:
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance is connected across <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> AC supply. The time taken by the current to change from its maximum value to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rms</mi></math> value is:
solution:
⇒i=i0sinωt

When i=i0

i0=i0sinωt1⇒ωt1=π2 …(1)

When i=i02

i02=i0sinωt2⇒ωt2=π4 …(2)

Time taken by current from maximum value to rms value, ⇒t1-t2=π2ω-π4ω=π4ω=π4×2πf

=18×50

=1400sec

=2.5 ms

subject: Physics
q: A series LCR circuit with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, is connected across an AC source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> supply. The power factor of the circuit would be ____.
question: A series LCR circuit with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, is connected across an AC source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> supply. The power factor of the circuit would be ____.
solution:
The capacitive reactance of the circuit is given by, 

Xc=1ωC=π2π×50×10-3=10 Ω

The inductive reactance of the circuit is given by, 

XL=ωL=2π×50×100π×10-3=10 Ω

From above calculations, it is clear that

XC=XL

∴ Power factor =RZ=RR=1

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Maximum power is dissipated in a circuit containing an inductor, a capacitor and a resistor connected in series with an AC source, when resonance occurs.

Statement II: Maximum power is dissipated in a circuit containing pure resistor due to zero phase difference between current and voltage.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Maximum power is dissipated in a circuit containing an inductor, a capacitor and a resistor connected in series with an AC source, when resonance occurs.

Statement II: Maximum power is dissipated in a circuit containing pure resistor due to zero phase difference between current and voltage.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false
solution:
The formula for impedance is given by 

Z=R2+XL-XC2

At resonance, XL=XC. So, Zmin=R

Power will be maximum when impedance is minimum. Thus the maximum power occurs in a purely resistive circuit.

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math> of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is applied to a parallel plate capacitor. The separation between the plates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The amplitude of the oscillating displacement current for the applied <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mtext>Take </mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math> of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is applied to a parallel plate capacitor. The separation between the plates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The amplitude of the oscillating displacement current for the applied <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mtext>Take </mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
From the given information,

C=∈0 A d=ϵ0×12×10-3 F
∴ XC=1ωC=2×10-32×50π×∈0=2×10-325×4π∈0 Ω
∴ XC=2×10-325×9×109=1825×106 Ω
∴ i0=V0XC=20×2518×10-6 A=27.47 μA.

The value of amplitude of displacement current will be same as value of amplitude of conventional current.
Hence option 3.

question: Two circuits are shown in figure (a) and (b). At a frequency of _______ rad s-1 the average power dissipated in one cycle will be the same in both the circuits.
solution:

Pavg=Vrms2R

vrms2Z2×R=vrms2R×1

subject: Physics
q: A LCR series circuit driven with $E_{rms} = 90$ V at frequency $f_d = 30$ Hz has resistance $R = 80\,\Omega$, an inductance with inductive reactance $X_L = 20.0\,\Omega$ and capacitance with capacitive reactance $X_C = 80.0\,\Omega$. The power factor of the circuit is _______.
question: A LCR series circuit driven with $E_{rms} = 90$ V at frequency $f_d = 30$ Hz has resistance $R = 80\,\Omega$, an inductance with inductive reactance $X_L = 20.0\,\Omega$ and capacitance with capacitive reactance $X_C = 80.0\,\Omega$. The power factor of the circuit is _______.
solution:
The impedance of the LCR series circuit is given by $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_C - X_L)^2}$.

Substituting the given values:

$Z = \sqrt{80^2 + (80 - 20)^2}$

$Z = \sqrt{80^2 + 60^2}$

$Z = \sqrt{6400 + 3600} = \sqrt{10000} = 100\,\Omega$

The power factor of the circuit is given by $\cos \phi = \dfrac{R}{Z}$.

$\cos \phi = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.8$

Answer: $0.8$

subject: Physics
q:
An ac current is represented as
$\mathrm{i}=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \mathrm{Amp}$
The r.m.s value of the current is
question:
An ac current is represented as
$\mathrm{i}=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \mathrm{Amp}$
The r.m.s value of the current is
solution:
$i=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\mathrm{i}^2=50+100 \cos ^2\left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$+(2)(5 \sqrt{2})(10) \cos \left(650 \pi t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\left\langle\mathrm{i}^2\right\rangle=50+\frac{100}{2}+0$
$\left. \lt \mathrm{i}^2\right\rangle=100$
$ \lt \mathrm{i} \gt =10 \mathrm{Amp}$

question: In an oscillating LC circuit the maximum charge on the capacitor is Q. The charge on the capacitor when the energy is stored equally between the electric and magnetic field is
solution: $q=\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}$

q: In an ac circuit, an inductor, a capacitor and a resistor are connected in series with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Impedance of this circuit is :
question: In an ac circuit, an inductor, a capacitor and a resistor are connected in series with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Impedance of this circuit is :
solution:
Recall the formula of impedance is in terms of resistance, Z=XL-XC2+R2, here, the condition of resonance, XL=XC, it means, 
Z=XL-XL2+R2=R

q: An arc lamp requires a direct current of 10 A at 80 V to function. If it is connected to a 220 V (rms), 50 Hz AC supply, the series inductor needed for it to work is close to:
question: An arc lamp requires a direct current of 10 A at 80 V to function. If it is connected to a 220 V (rms), 50 Hz AC supply, the series inductor needed for it to work is close to:
solution:
Given that the lamp draws a current of 10 A upon a 80 V D.C. supply, the resistance is,

R = 8010 = 8 Ω

When connected to an AC supply with an inductor in series, as shown in the figure above, the current,

 I = 10 A

and the voltage drop over the lamp is 80 V and VL over the inductance.

Applying the rules of L-R circuit,

VL2+802=2202

⇒VL=48400-6400=210 V

The Inductive reactance of the given inductor of inductance L, for the frequency of supply f,

XL=2πfL  ...(1)

The voltage drop across the inductance,

VL= I XL=210

⇒XL=2πfL=VLI=21010=21 Ω

Substituting values in equation (1)

L=212πf=212π×50=21100 π=0.065 H

q: A parallel plate capacitor has a capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi></math>. It is connected to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> ac supply with an angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The rms value of conduction current in the circuit and displacement current in the capacitor respectively are :
question: A parallel plate capacitor has a capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi></math>. It is connected to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> ac supply with an angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The rms value of conduction current in the circuit and displacement current in the capacitor respectively are :
solution:
By Maxwell's equation for a time varying voltage (AC voltage), both conduction and displacement currents are the same. Therefore,

Idisplacement=Iconduction=VrmsXC=Vrms1ωC=230×300×200×10−12=13.8 μA

The value of resistance in the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The self inductance in the circuit is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>.
question: The frequencies at which the current amplitude in an LCR series circuit becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math> times its maximum value, are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>212</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>232</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of resistance in the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The self inductance in the circuit is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>.
solution:
Bandwidth of RLC circuit is BW=f2-f1=R2πL

Or, BW=ω2-ω1=RL

BW=232-212=RL

Thus, self inductance in the circuit is L=520=250 mH

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor $C$ is first charged fully with potential difference of $V_{0}$ and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor is connected across an inductor having inductance $L$. In $t \mathrm{~s} \ 25 \%$ of the initial energy in the capacitor is transferred to the inductor. The value of $t$ is $\_\_\_\_$ s.
question: A capacitor $C$ is first charged fully with potential difference of $V_{0}$ and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor is connected across an inductor having inductance $L$. In $t \mathrm{~s} \ 25 \%$ of the initial energy in the capacitor is transferred to the inductor. The value of $t$ is $\_\_\_\_$ s.
solution:
In an LC circuit, energy oscillates sinusoidally.
Initial energy in capacitor is $E_0 = \frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$.
When 25% is transferred to the inductor, 75% remains in the capacitor.
Since energy in capacitor varies as $E_C(t) = E_0\cos^2(\omega t)$, we need $\cos^2(\omega t) = 0.75$.
This gives $\sin^2(\omega t) = 0.25$, so $\sin(\omega t) = 0.5$, yielding $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{6}$.
With $\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$, we get $t = \frac{\pi\sqrt{LC}}{6}$ seconds.

subject: Physics
q: A series LCR circuit is connected to an alternating source of emf E. The current amplitude at resonant frequency is $I_0$. If the value of resistance R becomes twice of its initial value then amplitude of current at resonance will be
question: A series LCR circuit is connected to an alternating source of emf E. The current amplitude at resonant frequency is $I_0$. If the value of resistance R becomes twice of its initial value then amplitude of current at resonance will be
solution:
Initially, $\mathrm{I}_0=\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{R}}$
Finally, $\mathrm{I}_0^1=\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{m}}}{2 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_0}{2}$

q: An LCR series circuit of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>62</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nF</mi></math> and resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, is connected to an A.C. source of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>. For maximum value of amplitude of current in circuit, the value of inductance is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>.<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mo>(</mo><mtext>Take</mtext><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: An LCR series circuit of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>62</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nF</mi></math> and resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, is connected to an A.C. source of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>. For maximum value of amplitude of current in circuit, the value of inductance is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>.<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mo>(</mo><mtext>Take</mtext><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Given here, f=2 kHz=2000 Hz, C=62.5 nF=62.5×10-9 F and R=50 Ω

Resonance frequency (amplitude of the current will be maximum in this case) is given by f=12πLC, here, L is the inductance.

Putting the values, 

2000 Hz=12πL×62.5×10-9

L=14π2×20002×62.5×10-9=0.1 H=100 mH

subject: Physics
q: An inductor coil stores 64 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates energy at the rate of 640 W when a current of 8 A is passed through it. If this coil is joined across an ideal battery, find the time constant of the circuit (in s).
question: An inductor coil stores 64 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates energy at the rate of 640 W when a current of 8 A is passed through it. If this coil is joined across an ideal battery, find the time constant of the circuit (in s).
solution:
Energy stored in an inductor, U=12LI2
⇒64=12L(8)2 ⇒L=2 H
and power dissipated in a resistor, P=I2R
⇒640=(8)2R ⇒R=10 Ω
Time constant of the LR DC circuit is,
τ=LR=210=0.2 s

solution:
The average power dissipated is,<P>=IVcosϕ

=102×202×cos60°

=50 W
